plainMeaning: Countless are the shrines among the Vyantara and Jyotiṣka gods, countless the Jina-temples and countless the Jina-images — bowing to them all, we have no fear of a bad rebirth.
recitation: asaṅkhyā vyantara joisī, asaṅkhyā jinhar; asaṅkhyā paḍimā jinataṇī, namie nahi durgati ḍar

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit a- (not) + saṅkhyā (number, reckoning); Prakrit asaṅkhejja — 'incalculable'.
wordByWord.notes: Anaphoric: it heads each of the three clauses, hammering the shift from the previous verse's exact crores to the uncountable.

wordByWord.gloss: [shrines in the realms of] the Vyantara gods
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit vyantara, 'intermediate' — the second of the four classes of devas, dwelling in the intermediate spaces of the tiryag-loka.
wordByWord.notes: Here the deva-class name stands by metonymy for the eternal Jina-shrines housed in its realms.

wordByWord.gloss: [shrines in the realms of] the Jyotiṣka (luminary) gods
wordByWord.etymology: Prakritized form of Sanskrit jyotiṣka (jyotis 'light'); Gujarati joisī — the star/sun/moon deities.
wordByWord.notes: Paired with vyantara as a dvandva; both deva-realms hold śāśvata caityas, the unstated head-noun of the clause.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit jina + gṛha ('house') → Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa jiṇaharo → Gujarati jinhar, 'house of the Jina'.
wordByWord.notes: Book-spelling kept verbatim; a glossary term (jina-caitya) used here, not re-defined.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit paḍimā from Sanskrit pratimā ('image, likeness'); the same word as jina-pratimā.
wordByWord.notes: Feminine; agrees with the following possessive jinataṇī.

wordByWord.etymology: jina + Old Gujarati genitive postposition taṇī (feminine of taṇaüṁ, 'belonging to'), from Apabhraṁśa tanaya-.
wordByWord.notes: Possessive marker carrying feminine concord with paḍimā; a hallmark of medieval Gujarati verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati namvuṁ from Sanskrit root nam- ('to bend, salute'); first-person plural / hortative.
wordByWord.notes: The verse's single finite verb, gathering all three innumerable objects into one act of homage.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit na + hi (emphatic); Gujarati negation 'not at all'.

wordByWord.gloss: evil destiny, bad rebirth (in hell or as an animal)
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit dur- (bad) + gati (course of existence); Prakrit duggai — the naraka and tiryañca gatis.
Gujarati ḍar from Sanskrit/Prakrit dara ('fear'), via root dṛ- ('to dread').
wordByWord.notes: Predicate of the second clause: nahi durgati ḍar = '[there is] no dread of an evil rebirth'.

literalTranslation: Innumerable [shrines] among the Vyantara and Jyotiṣka [gods], innumerable Jina-temples; innumerable images of the Jina — we bow [to them]; no fear of an evil rebirth.
idiomaticTranslation: Beyond all counting are the shrines among the Vyantara and luminary gods, beyond counting the temples of the Jina, beyond counting His sacred images — to all of them we bow, and dread of an evil rebirth falls away.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse executes a deliberate rhetorical leap. The preceding stanza still trades in figures one can write down — 'sixty lakh, eighty-nine crore, and thirteen hundred crore' images. Here the poet abandons arithmetic altogether: the single word asaṅkhyā, struck three times like a bell, declares the shrines of the Vyantara and Jyotiṣka realms, the Jina-temples, and the Jina-images to be simply uncountable. The movement is from the large-but-finite to the genuinely incalculable, so that the worshipper's mind, having tried and failed to total the previous verse, is now released into pure awe. Note the compression: where earlier verses named places and numbers, this one names only classes of the sacred — deva-realm shrine, temple, image — and lets asaṅkhyā do all the work. The whole cosmic census of the middle-world divine realms is folded into a single line. Then comes the turn. Three clauses of objects are resolved by one small verb, namie — 'we bow' — and the act of homage is immediately given its fruit: nahi durgati ḍar, 'no fear of an evil rebirth.' The verse thus completes a tiny self-contained liturgy: an overwhelming object of reverence, a single humble gesture, and a soteriological reward. The asyndetic piling of asaṅkhyā … asaṅkhyā … asaṅkhyā against the lone namie enacts the very humility it preaches — the countless above, the one bow below.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on two settled Jain teachings. First, the śāśvata-caitya doctrine: the realms of the Vyantara and Jyotiṣka gods, like the other heavens, contain eternal, uncreated Jina-temples and images that have always existed — hence 'innumerable' is doctrinally exact, not hyperbole. Bowing to them is bowing to the Jina Himself through His caitya. Second, the law of gati: beings transmigrate through deva, manuṣya, tiryañca and naraka destinies, of which the last two are durgati. The claim that reverence dissolves 'fear of durgati' is the bhakti-corollary of karma theory: devotional homage to the Jina-image generates puṇya and steadies samyaktva, and a soul firm in right faith and merit does not slide into the lower gatis. The verse therefore binds cosmology (countless shrines) to ethics (no evil rebirth) in a single breath.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited at dawn, the verse trains the mind to stop counting and start bowing: one need not enumerate or even visit these shrines — a single sincere namaskāra mentally embraces them all. Its practical promise is reassurance: the reciter rises from the night's pratikramaṇa not in dread of consequences but in the confidence that devotion to the Jina turns the soul away from durgati.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 4.1–4 (the four classes of devas: bhavanapati, vyantara, jyotiṣka, vaimānika)
Tattvārtha-sūtra 2.6 (the four gatis; naraka and tiryañca as durgati)
Bṛhat-saṅgrahaṇī (Jaina cosmographic census of śāśvata caityas and bimbas in the deva-realms)

sources.title: Vyantara — 13 definitions
sources.consultedFor: Confirming Vyantara as the intermediate deva-class of Jain cosmology, glossed for વ્યંતર.

sources.title: Jyotishka, Jyotiṣka — 22 definitions
sources.consultedFor: Identifying જોઇસી as the Prakritized jyotiṣka (luminary) deva-class.

sources.title: Pratima, Pratimā — 40 definitions
sources.consultedFor: Etymology and sense of પડિમા (paḍimā) as Jina-image from Sanskrit pratimā.

sources.title: Durgati, Dur-gati — 19 definitions
sources.consultedFor: Glossing દુર્ગતિ as bad rebirth (naraka/tiryañca gati) in the Jain sense.

notes: The three clauses share an unstated head-noun: vyantara/joisī name the deva-realms by metonymy for the eternal Jina-shrines (caityas) they contain, paralleling jinhar (temples) and paḍimā (images). Book spellings જિનહર and પડિમા are preserved verbatim. 'jinataṇī' uses the Old/medieval Gujarati feminine genitive postposition taṇī, agreeing with the feminine paḍimā. The final clause is elliptical — 'namie [→] nahi durgati ḍar' reads idiomatically as 'by bowing, no fear of durgati'; an alternative parsing 'we bow [so that there is] no fear of durgati' yields the same devotional sense. No variant readings required adjudication for this verse.
